title: YCbCr: Luminance and Chrominance
concept: YCbCr splits an image into a luma channel (Y, overall brightness and detail) and two chroma channels (Cb and Cr, color information) — because human eyes notice far more detail in brightness than in color, which is exactly what video and image compression exploit.

exercises.prompt: Which do your eyes notice more precisely: small changes in brightness, or small changes in color?

options.text: Small changes in brightness — human vision is far more sensitive to brightness detail than color detail.
Small changes in color — brightness barely matters to human vision.
Both are noticed with exactly equal precision.

exercises.explain: Right — this brightness-first sensitivity is the whole reason YCbCr exists.

cards.title: An old black-and-white photo still 'makes sense'
cards.body: Strip all the color out of a photo and you can usually still recognize every shape, face, and object — just without color. Strip out the shapes and keep only blotches of color, and the photo becomes unrecognizable. Brightness carries almost all the detail; color is more like a light wash on top.

cards.title: Y, Cb, and Cr
cards.body: YCbCr splits a color image into three channels: Y (luma) is the brightness and detail — basically the grayscale version of the image. Cb is the blue-difference chroma, and Cr is the red-difference chroma — together they carry the color information, separately from the brightness.

cards.title: A pencil sketch with a light color wash
cards.body: Think of Y as a detailed pencil sketch capturing every edge and shadow, and Cb/Cr as a thin, low-detail wash of color painted loosely over the top. Because your eyes are lenient about exactly where the color wash lines up, that wash doesn't need nearly as much precision as the sketch underneath it.

cards.title: Storing color at lower resolution than brightness
cards.body: This brightness-first sensitivity lets video and image formats use chroma subsampling: keep Y at full resolution, but store Cb and Cr at a quarter of the detail — say, one color sample for every 2x2 block of pixels. Viewers rarely notice, but the file gets dramatically smaller. This trick, common in JPEG and video codecs, only works because color is split out from brightness in the first place.

cards.title: Formats don't just save RGB directly
cards.body: It's easy to assume a JPEG or video file stores red, green, and blue values straight from the camera. Most actually convert to YCbCr first, precisely so they can compress the chroma channels harder than the luma channel. If everything stayed in RGB, there would be no clean way to spend fewer bits on color than on brightness.

cards.title: Same picture, very different storage budgets
cards.body: Splitting brightness from color changes how much data each part needs.

left.text:
Red, Green, Blue.
All three channels stored at full resolution.
No channel is 'more important' than another.

right.text:
Y at full resolution.
Cb and Cr often stored at reduced resolution.
Big file-size savings, minimal visible loss.

exercises.prompt: In YCbCr, which channel alone gives you essentially a grayscale version of the image?
exercises.explain: Correct — Y is the brightness channel, which is exactly what a grayscale image shows.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Human eyes notice a loss of detail in brightness far more than a loss of detail in color.
exercises.explain: True — that's the entire justification for storing color (Cb/Cr) at lower resolution than brightness (Y).

exercises.sentence: Storing the Cb and Cr channels at lower resolution than Y, with little visible quality loss, is called chroma ___.
exercises.explain: Subsampling is correct — it's the technique of sampling color less densely than brightness.

exercises.prompt: Which reasoning for why video compression converts frames to YCbCr is accurate?

options.text: It separates brightness from color, so color detail can be reduced much more than brightness detail without viewers noticing.
YCbCr uses fewer total channels than RGB, so it's automatically smaller no matter what.
YCbCr is only used for black-and-white video, never color.

exercises.explain: Exactly — the savings come from subsampling chroma harder than luma, not from having fewer channels.

exercises.prompt: A streaming app needs to shrink video files while keeping them looking sharp. Which strategy best uses what YCbCr makes possible?

options.text: Convert to YCbCr, keep the Y (luma) channel at full resolution, and store the Cb/Cr (chroma) channels at reduced resolution.
Stay in RGB and reduce the resolution of the red, green, and blue channels equally.
Convert to YCbCr and reduce the resolution of Y while keeping Cb and Cr at full detail.
Discard the Cb and Cr channels entirely and keep only Y.

exercises.explain: Right — reducing chroma resolution while preserving luma is exactly the brightness-first compression trick YCbCr enables.
